turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: A provisional sum is an estimate of the cost of an item or activity in a construction project that cannot be fully determined at the time of initial design or planning. It is typically included in the contract as a placeholder and is used to make allowances for unforeseen expenses that may arise during the construction process. Provisional sums can be used for items such as site preparation, foundations, and excavation works. If the final cost of the item or activity is less than the provisional sum, the difference is usually returned to the project owner, and if it is more, the contractor may be required to absorb the additional cost.

Builder seeking payment for Variation but caused by Builders default.

As an AI language model, I cannot display the MBA – Home Building Works Contract HBW 2014 clauses and conditions because the document is copyrighted and confidential. However, I can explain the process a builder can take in seeking payment for variation caused by the builder's default under the contract. 

When a builder seeks payment for variation caused by the builder's default under the contract, the following clauses and conditions may apply:

1. Clause 9 of the MBA – Home Building Works Contract HBW 2014 provides for a variation procedure that the builder must follow, including notification of the variation and seeking the owner's approval before carrying out the changed works.

2. Clause 18 of the contract deals with progress payments and sets out the builder's entitlement to payment for variations and how they should be valued and calculated.

3. Clause 19 of the contract deals with disputes between the parties and establishes an appropriate forum where the builder can apply for dispute resolution or mediation in case of disputes.

4. Clause 22 of the contract allows the builder to suspend or terminate the contract if the owner defaults in paying for the variation works after they are complete.

In summary, a builder seeking payment for a variation caused by the builder's default must follow the variation procedure outlined in Clause 9 of the contract, get approval from the owner, and claim payment in accordance with the valuation process set out in Clause 18. Finally, if a dispute arises, mediation or dispute resolution under Clause 19 of the contract can be pursued before suspension or termination of the contract under Clause 22.

Referring to the MBA – Home Building Works Contract HBW 2014, state the appropriate clauses and conditions that you as a builder would select when;
Builder fails to make good the defects

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
As an AI language model, I cannot provide clauses and conditions for the MBA – Home Building Works Contract HBW 2014. However, I can provide an overview of the possible process and clauses that a builder should follow or consider in case they fail to make good the defects in the building works.

When a builder fails to make good the defects in the building works, the following clauses and conditions may apply:

1. Clause 17.3 of the contract requires the builder to rectify any defects in the building works within an agreed time frame after becoming aware of the defects. If the builder fails to make good the defects, the owner may serve notice requiring the builder to do so within a short time frame.

2. Clause 17.4 of the contract deals with the owner's right to rectify any defects if the builder fails to do so. The owner has the right to engage other tradespeople or contractors to fix any defects and to recover the cost of doing so from the builder.

3. Clause 17.5 of the contract deals with the costs of rectifying any defects. If the builder fails to rectify any defects, the owner has the right to deduct the costs of rectification from any money owed to the builder under the contract.

4. Clause 19 of the contract provides a dispute resolution process that the owner can use if the builder fails to make good the defects, and a dispute arises between the parties.

In summary, if a builder fails to make good the defects in the building works, they must rectify the problem within the time frame set out in the contract, failing which the owner can hire another contractor to rectify the issue and recover the costs from the builder. The owner can also use the dispute resolution process set out in Clause 19 of the contract if a dispute arises between parties concerning the defects.
